sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage: diagnosis and initial management
sources.version: NICE NG126; last updated and reviewed 2026-06-17
sources.population: Women, trans men, and non-binary people with pain, bleeding, or other complications in early pregnancy up to 13 completed weeks.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, emergency, gynaecology, and early pregnancy assessment services.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for early-pregnancy pain, bleeding, ectopic-pregnancy warning signs, ultrasound, and hCG assessment; partial for uncomplicated pregnancy symptoms and not directly validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Overview and recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.4 — early pregnancy assessment services
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines the up-to-13-week complication population and services offering specialist assessment, ultrasound, serum hCG review, and timely referral.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1–1.4.12 — presentation, assessment and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Lists pain, amenorrhoea, bleeding, gastrointestinal symptoms, dizziness or syncope, shoulder-tip pain, urinary symptoms, tenderness, haemodynamic signs, pregnancy testing, and escalation criteria.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5.1–1.7.6 — ultrasound for pregnancy location and viability
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines clinician-performed transvaginal or selected transabdominal ultrasound and cautions against overinterpreting one early scan or LMP alone.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.8.1–1.8.10 — hCG in pregnancy of unknown location
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires symptoms to take priority over hCG, limits hCG interpretation to appropriate clinical use, and requires clear worsening-symptom access and follow-up.

sources.issuing_organisation: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists
sources.exact_document_title: The Initial Management of Chronic Pelvic Pain — Green-top Guideline No. 41
sources.version: Second edition; Green-top Guideline No. 41; third edition in development
sources.population: Women presenting for the first time with chronic pelvic pain of at least 6 months that is not pregnancy-related.
sources.clinical_setting: General gynaecology and primary-care referral assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for the chronic non-pregnancy subset only; old, international, and partial for acute, pregnancy-related, infectious, or emergency pelvic-pain presentations.

exact_sections.heading: 1. Purpose and Scope; 2. Background; 5. Initial Assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines chronic pelvic pain as a symptom rather than a diagnosis and frames initial assessment around the woman’s concerns, function, and multifactorial context.

exact_sections.heading: 5.1 History; 5.2 Examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Specifies pain pattern, menstrual, bladder, bowel, movement, posture, function and medication history plus abdominal and pelvic examination for focal tenderness or structural findings.

exact_sections.heading: 6.1 Screening for Infection; 6.2 Transvaginal Scanning and MRI
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-selected STI sampling when PID is suspected and transvaginal ultrasound for adnexal masses and selected chronic pelvic-pain pathology.
